countries: Australia (Great Barrier Reef, Lord Howe Island)
nativeRange.habitat: Adults frequent lagoon and seaward reefs with hard substrate at depths of roughly 0–90 m; juveniles are often abundant in tide pools and shallow inshore areas, sometimes near freshwater runoffs

decorPreferences: ample open swimming space along the length of the tank
rockwork crevices and caves for shelter
long footprint (6 ft+) preferred over tall depth
tightly fitted lid recommended when mixed with other species

diet.notes: Primarily grazes filamentous and macroalgae from live rock; dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based preparations are generally advised at least three times per week. Has been documented accepting flake/pellet foods and small meaty items such as mysis shrimp in mixed-community feedings. Frequent algae offerings have been reported to strengthen immune response and reduce conspecific aggression.

avoidWith: established conspecifics added later (introduce a small group simultaneously)
very aggressive tankmates that out-compete it for food

summary: Indo-Pacific surgeonfish with a silvery body and six vertical black bars (hence 'convict'). Generally considered an active grazer suited to mature reef systems with substantial open swimming space; have been known to be harassed by other tangs.
careNotes:
The Convict Tang (Acanthurus triostegus) is widely distributed across the Indo-Pacific and into the Eastern Pacific, and tends to reach roughly 20–27 cm in well-kept aquariums; FishBase records a maximum of 27 cm TL, while retail sources commonly report ~8 in (20 cm) for typical specimens.

A spacious tank with a long footprint is generally advised — source recommendations span 125 gal (473 L, Reef Chasers) and 130 gal (492 L, LiveAquaria) at the entry-level end to 700 L (ReefApp), which is the figure used here on the cautious-larger side given the species' active swimming behaviour and the prose-level emphasis on 'ample swimming space' and the fact that this species 'swims great distances every day' in the wild.

Water quality should be stable and well-oxygenated within standard reef parameters (SG ~1.020–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12). Temperature is commonly recommended around 23–27°C, with LiveAquaria stating 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) at the lower end and FishBase's modeled preferred range reaching up to ~28.9°C at the upper end.

Diet should lean heavily on marine algae; offering dried seaweed and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week is generally advised. Like many surgeonfishes, the Convict Tang is susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) in poor water quality or on a vitamin-deficient diet; a varied algae-rich diet (sometimes with vitamin supplementation) tends to mitigate this risk.

The Convict Tang is generally considered reef-safe; well-fed individuals tend not to nip at corals, though occasional sampling has been reported when algae is scarce. Temperament is best described as semi-aggressive — generally peaceable with non-tang reef fish once established, but commonly harassed by (and harassing toward) other Acanthurus and similarly shaped surgeonfishes. Multiple Convict Tangs can sometimes coexist if a small group is introduced simultaneously to a sufficiently large system.

Acanthurids possess a sharp caudal-peduncle blade (the 'scalpel') used for defense; it is not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates during conflicts. Handle with care during transfers.

In the wild, Acanthurus triostegus forms spawning aggregations and reaches sexual maturity at ~8.8 cm; FishBase does not report protogynous (female-first) sex change for this species, in contrast to many other acanthurids.

Source cross-check notes: Adult size — LiveAquaria 8 in (20 cm), Reef Chasers 8 in (20 cm), ReefApp 26 cm, FishBase 27 cm TL; used a 20–27 cm aquarium-realistic range. Minimum tank — 125 gal (473 L) to 130 gal (492 L) at retail, 700 L at ReefApp; per the tank-size cautious-larger rule selected 700 L to accommodate the active swimming behaviour repeatedly emphasised in prose. Temperature spread 22.2–28.9°C across sources; averaged to 23–27°C.
breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity in available hobby sources. In the wild, Acanthurus triostegus forms broadcast-spawning aggregations producing pelagic eggs; sexual maturity is reached at around 8.8 cm. The extended planktonic larval (acronurus) stage and the species' aggregation-spawning behaviour make captive breeding considered impractical in typical home aquaria.

Provided scientific name (Acanthurus triostegus), max size (8 in), tank minimum (130 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), diet (herbivore, marine vegetation), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (yes), husbandry notes on conspecific introductions and seaweed feeding for immunity.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked species identity, family (Acanthuridae) and order (Acanthuriformes), max size (27 cm TL), depth range (0–90 m), native range (Indo-Pacific and Eastern Pacific from lower Gulf of California to Panama including Revillagigedo, Cocos, Clipperton and Galapagos), preferred temperature (23.4–28.9°C, mean ~27.6°C), habitat (lagoon/seaward reefs with hard substrate; juveniles in tide pools), spawning aggregations, sexual maturity at 8.8 cm, generation time ~3.1 years, and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010).

Confirmed adult size (26 cm), minimum tank (700 L), temperature (~26°C), reef-safe status, herbivorous diet with macroalgae and spirulina, peaceful temperament outside its own genus, need for ample swimming space and hiding places.

additional.name: Reef Chasers Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~8 in), minimum tank (125 gal / 473 L) with wider-than-tall footprint, semi-aggressive toward other tangs, caudal-spine defense, HLLE susceptibility, herbivorous grazing on live-rock algae supplemented with nori/leafy greens, netted-lid recommendation in mixed setups.

fish.breedingNotes: Not documented in captivity. Wild populations form broadcast-spawning aggregations producing pelagic eggs; sexual maturity reached at ~8.8 cm. The extended planktonic acronurus larval stage and aggregation-spawning behaviour make captive breeding impractical in typical home aquaria.
